name: Event-Loop Scheduling
summary: Scheduler family in which a single thread maintains a set of ready tasks plus a registration of I/O interests and dispatches them as the OS reports readiness/completion; powers JavaScript runtimes, libuv, Python asyncio, single-threaded Tokio, and the thread-per-core io_uring runtimes (Glommio, Monoio).

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
An event-loop runtime maintains a set of ready tasks and I/O interests. When the OS reports readiness or completion, the runtime wakes the corresponding tasks. This model powers JavaScript runtimes (V8/libuv, JSC/Bun), libuv-based systems generally, Python `asyncio`, single-threaded Tokio runtimes, and many embedded async executors.

Event-loop schedulers are efficient for I/O-bound workloads but must prevent CPU-bound tasks from starving the loop. They need explicit blocking pools, cooperative yielding, or preemption support.

sections.title: Thread-per-core io_uring runtimes
sections.content:
A distinctive Rust subfamily is the **thread-per-core io_uring runtimes** — **Glommio** (Datadog) and **Monoio** (Bytedance). Each worker thread runs its own independent single-threaded event loop bound to a specific CPU core via affinity, with its own `io_uring` instance for asynchronous I/O. Inter-core coordination is by explicit message passing through bounded channels rather than work-stealing across a shared scheduler.

Distinct from Tokio's M:N work-stealing model where futures freely migrate between worker threads: Glommio/Monoio futures are pinned to one core for their lifetime, eliminating cross-core synchronisation on the hot path. The trade-off is sharp — perfect cache locality and zero shared-state contention, at the cost of needing to manually balance work across cores when the workload isn't naturally per-connection or per-shard.

sections.title: Primary/worker reactor topology
sections.content:
A different topology layers on top of single-threaded eventloops: a **Primary Reactor** accepts connections on listen sockets and redistributes each accepted socket to one of N **Worker Reactors**, each of which runs its own independent eventloop in its own OS thread. ActiveJ Eventloop + Workers (`data/activej-eventloop`) is the JVM canonical example. Workers don't share mutable heap state; cross-worker communication is by explicit message-passing.

Distinct from goroutine-shaped runtimes (shared heap, work-stealing) and from full actor systems (mailbox + supervision per actor): each worker is itself an arbitrary-code eventloop, but inter-worker isolation is structural.

sections.title: All-in-one runtimes
sections.content:
**Bun** (`data/bun`) is a notable production data point: a Zig-implemented all-in-one runtime over JavaScriptCore with a custom Zig I/O loop, supporting `io_uring` on Linux and exposing Node-compatible APIs (`fs`, `http`, `child_process`, `cluster`) plus Web-standard APIs (`fetch`, `WebSocket`, `Response`) on the same eventloop.

The lesson generalises: **a single eventloop is the smallest practical concurrency runtime** and the easiest to make portable across OS interfaces — readiness (`epoll`, `kqueue`), completion (IOCP, `io_uring`), or hybrid models all fit the same surface.

sections.title: Trade-offs
